availability.p:
Published under a
Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 32 [Bnf 12926 Transcription - English Translation]
div.p: The third reason why this crime of sacrificing the innocent should be overlooked when it cannot be cured except by war: and when some innocents cannot be freed without harm to other innocents, is this: namely when the guilty cannot be distinguished from the innocent, which manifestly happens in wars: on account of the confusion and disorder with which they are filled.

These things are proven from the words of the Lord
Matthew 13 , when he forbids the weeds to be pulled up lest perhaps the wheat also be pulled up at the same time. Do you wish (the apostles say) that we go and gather them? And he said:
No, lest gathering the weeds you uproot the wheat at the same time. Let both grow together until the harvest
and so on. Lest wanting to pull up the weeds you pull up the wheat also. Wheat signifies the innocent and pious, or those who can return to a mindset worthy of God. However, lest harm be done to these innocents: the Lord forbids the wicked to be uprooted, but defers their punishment to the day of judgment and this for no other reason than that the pious or innocent not perish with the impious and guilty.

Wherefore the Lord explains thus.
He who sows the good seed, these are the children of the kingdom: but the weeds are the children of wickedness. The enemy who sowed them is the devil. The harvest truly is the end of the age. And the harvesters are the angels .
Therefore the Son of Man will send his angels, and they will gather from his kingdom all scandals, and those who do iniquity. And they will cast them into the furnace of fire . Therefore Christ does not want but prohibits the guilty to be judged / punished or pulled up by men: as often as they cannot be distinguished from the innocent and thus without danger to them. And this is another case in which the Lord has reserved to himself the judgment of sinners.

Which the gloss teaches, explaining those words:
Lord, do you wish us? It consults (he says) the justice of God whether he should do it, and whether God wishes this to be the office of men. But the truth responded: man cannot know in this life what sort of person he who now errs will be: and what his error may confer on the good: and therefore they are not to be removed: lest perhaps those who are going to be good be killed: or lest it stand in the way of the good, whom they benefit . And below:
Then truly it will be done opportunely: when there is no longer time for changing life and profiting others: and then he says it will be done not by men but by angels . This is the gloss.

From which it is manifestly gathered, that those who in the proposed case kill the guilty with loss and harm to the innocents whom the storm of war will necessarily involve in destruction: they act impiously, usurping the office which the Lord reserved to himself and the angels. Thus teaches
Chrysostom on Matthew chapter 1, homily 1 . not in the imperfect work
see (he says) how illicit it is to usurp a work that has not been entrusted by God
and so on. And
Saint Thomas 2a 2ae, q. 108, art. 1, ad 1m . where it is said:
that if anyone exercises vengeance outside the order of divine institution, he usurps to himself what belongs to God and sins against God . This is what he says.

The same asserts
Saint Thomas in various places
discussing the same parable: namely:
that the Lord commanded to abstain from the uprooting of weeds in order to spare the wheat, that is, the good: which indeed happens because the wicked cannot be killed without the good being killed at the same time: either because they lie hidden among the good: or because they have many followers so that they cannot be killed without danger to the good, as
Augustine says against Parmenianus . Whence the Lord teaches that it is better to allow the wicked to live: and vengeance to be reserved: until the final judgment: than that the good be killed at the same time. But when from the killing of the wicked no danger threatens the good, but rather protection and safety: then the wicked can be killed lawfully . These are his words exactly.
2a 2ae, q. 10, art. 8, ad 1m . and
q. 11, art. 3, ad 3m . and
q. 64, art. 2, ad 1m . and
q. 108, art. 1, ad 5m . and
art. 3, ad 1m . and
4th Sentences, dist. 13, q. 2, art. 3, ad 3m . and
book 3, c. 146, at the end, against the Gentiles . And quodlibet 10, art. 5, ad 2m.

The same teaches on Matthew
expounding that parable of the Lord, where he gives four reasons for this precept, namely that the wicked should not be pulled up lest the pious be endangered. First, for the exercise of the patience of the good. Second, because those who are wicked will perhaps be converted from weeds into wheat: and detesting their impious life they will be converted to the Lord: and from wolves they will become lambs just as we have seen in Paul. The gloss also gave these two reasons. Third, because many seem impious and yet their mind lacks impiety. Fourth, because if some impious person is very powerful: he cannot be punished without disturbance of all divine and human affairs.

An example of this most holy teaching was provided by Saint Ambrose, who inveighed most bitterly against the Emperor Theodosius, otherwise pious, because in an angry spirit, he ordered the people of Thessalonica to be gathered in the forum under a sought pretext: and because they had killed a judge sent by him, he ordered both guilty and innocent to be indiscriminately slaughtered, which we read
in the ecclesiastical history, book 11, chapter 13 .
More extensively in book 9, chapter 30 . in
the tripartite history: Thessalonica (it says) is a great and populous city in which when there was sedition, certain judges were stoned and killed. Hence Theodosius, indignant, did not restrain the weakness of his anger: but ordered unjust swords to be drawn against all, and innocents to be killed along with the guilty; seven thousand men are reported to have been killed thus
and so on. This is what it says.

But in our case not seven thousand but seventy thousand: indeed seven hundred myriads of men, it is necessary to kill: among whom perhaps nearly all are most innocent before God, at least of those crimes on account of which we judge the war to be just (namely) the sacrificing of infants and the eating of human flesh. Again, this truth is proven from the decrees of jurists. For we read in
the law "absentem", ff de penis where it says
It is better (some say holier) for the crime of a guilty person to go unpunished: than for an innocent person to be condemned .

Where Bartolus says that if it is clear to the judge that someone did not commit the crime, or that he did not commit the crime, or that he is not culpable: the judge cannot condemn him even from his own true confession, as in
l. inde Neratius at the end . and
the following law ff. ad l. aquil . And this is what is said,
that the judge should seek the defense of the accused , as in
ff. de quaestio., l. 9 at the end . and
l. si non defendatur, ff. de poenis . The same is understood when there is doubt about guilt.

So also says
Chrysostom on Matthew, chapter 1, homily 1, in the imperfect work .
In an uncertain matter (he says) it is better that a prostitute, once established, escape: than that an innocent die. For it is more just that an unjust person justly escape: than that a just person unjustly perish. Because even if a guilty person once escapes he can perish again: but an innocent, if he once perishes: can no longer be recalled . This is Chrysostom.

From these words of Chrysostom you see that our opinion is most true, and that it is a sin worthy of eternal death: to afflict and kill innocents so that the guilty may be punished, which is against justice. Likewise, the greatest care must be taken lest under the pretext of punishing the guilty, the innocent be punished, as in
l. 2 at the end, c. de his qui latro occul . Where Baldus says: that innocents should neither be seized nor burdened with expenses nor in any other thing. He cites
l. 1, § tribuni in that title . The same one says that whoever causes someone to be seized who afterward appears innocent, should then be punished with a similar punishment: and thus he says:
on account of this a certain man in Perugia was beheaded, which note . This is Baldus.

It also applies because an unjust condition should not be imposed on one person by another.
ff. de regul. jur., l. nemo debet . And punishment should hold only its authors.
l. sancimus, C. de penis, l. si pena, ff. eo titulo, l. ab maritorum, C. ne vxor pro marito , and
c. illud, 11, q. 3 . and
c. Romana, de sen. excommunicationis, lib. 6 . and c. Placuit, and c. crimen, and c. nullius. and
c. judaei, 1, q. 4 . and
c. peruenit, de his quae fi. Maiore par. cap .

The same is proven from
the canonical decrees in c. denique, 14, q. 5 . where Ambrose says:
finally, if one person cannot be helped unless another is harmed: it is more fitting that neither be helped than that one be burdened , and so on. Where
the Archdeacon says. and in
c. ipsa pietas, 23, q. 4 . that no one should grant aid to another from which directly and necessarily follows harm to another: and rightly, because no one should be provided for with another's loss or injury, as in c. primum there
one must not lie in the third manner
and so on 22, q. 2. and 11, q. 3, si quis vero. And in the rule
no one should be enriched with another's
and so on. And l. jure nature, ff. de regu. jur. where the text says
by natural law it is equitable that no one be made richer with another's detriment and injury .
